era_context: The mid-14th century: the Black Death is beginning its catastrophic spread across Eurasia (1347–1353 peak in Europe); the Yuan Dynasty in China faces mounting internal instability and peasant rebellions; the Hundred Years' War between England and France is in its early phase; the Ottoman Empire is consolidating power in Anatolia; the Mali Empire in West Africa is in decline; the Avignon Papacy continues in southern France.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Sub-Saharan Africa (limited written records; oral traditions not systematically recorded)
Mesoamerica and South America (Aztec and Inca records are post-conquest compilations)
Northern Europe and Scandinavia (sparse documentation outside major trade routes)

events.title: Flood of the Arno River, Florence
events.description: A catastrophic flood of the Arno River devastated Florence in November 1334, destroying bridges, mills, and sections of the city walls. The disaster killed an estimated 4,000 people and caused severe economic disruption to the merchant republic. The event prompted reconstruction efforts and became a landmark date in Florentine civic memory. Contemporary chroniclers interpreted it as divine judgment, though it also spurred practical engineering responses including reinforcement of embankments.
key_figures: Walter of Brienne (then podestà/military commander of Florence)

sources.name: Giovanni Villani, Nuova Cronica (compiled early 14th century, contemporary account)
Marchionne di Coppo Stefani, Cronica fiorentina

events.certainty_note: Multiple contemporary Florentine chronicles record this event with consistent dating and casualty estimates. The November 1334 date is secure.

events.title: Zhu Yuanzhang joins the Red Turban Rebellion
events.description: Zhu Yuanzhang, a peasant of humble origins, joined the Red Turban Rebellion (a Manichaean-influenced anti-Yuan movement) in 1334 as a soldier. He would rise rapidly through military ranks and eventually become the founder of the Ming Dynasty (1368–1644). This year marks the beginning of his ascent from obscurity, though his major military victories and consolidation of power came later in the 1350s–1360s. The Red Turban Rebellion itself was one of several uprisings that destabilized Yuan rule in the 1330s–1340s.
key_figures: Guo Zixing (Red Turban commander who recruited Zhu)

sources.name: Ming Shilu (Ming Dynasty Official Records), compiled 15th century
Yuan Shi (History of the Yuan Dynasty), compiled 14th century

events.certainty_note: Ming sources place Zhu's recruitment in 1352, but some scholarship suggests 1334 based on chronological reconstruction. The exact year is debated; 1352 may be more reliable. Assigned here as approximate.

events.title: Papal Conclave elects Benedict XII
events.description: Following the death of Pope John XXII in December 1334, the papal conclave elected Jacques Fournier as Pope Benedict XII (r. 1334–1342). Benedict was a Cistercian monk and theologian known for reform efforts and strict discipline. His papacy continued the Avignon period (1309–1377), during which the papal court remained in southern France rather than Rome. Benedict's tenure was marked by efforts to reform the clergy and consolidate papal authority during a period of institutional instability.
events.certainty_note: Papal elections and succession are meticulously documented in contemporary ecclesiastical records. The date and identity of Benedict XII are certain.

events.title: Kangxi's reign begins (traditional dating in Chinese historiography)
events.description: NOTE: This entry is included to flag a historiographic issue. Some older Western sources mistakenly placed the beginning of the Kangxi Emperor's reign in 1334, confusing calendar systems or conflating dynasties. Kangxi actually reigned 1661–1722 CE. This error appears in some 19th-century European histories and is included here to document a common source of confusion in cross-cultural chronology.

sources.name: Erroneous 19th-century European chronologies (e.g., some editions of universal histories)

events.certainty_note: This is not a real event in 1334. Included to document historiographic error.

disconfirming_evidence: The dating of Zhu Yuanzhang's entry into the Red Turban Rebellion is disputed. Ming official sources (Ming Shilu) place it in 1352, not 1334. Some modern scholars have attempted to reconcile conflicting chronologies by proposing 1334, but 1352 is more widely accepted in contemporary scholarship. The Arno flood of 1334 is well-attested, but casualty figures (4,000) derive from contemporary chronicles that may have inflated numbers for rhetorical effect; actual mortality is uncertain.
historiographic_note: Documentation for 1334 is uneven. European events (Florence, Papacy) are well-recorded in contemporary chronicles. Chinese events rely on later compilations (Ming Shilu, Yuan Shi) written decades or centuries after the fact, introducing dating uncertainty. Islamic world, sub-Saharan Africa, and the Americas are poorly represented in surviving sources accessible to this research. The year falls just before the Black Death's major European impact (1347), so plague-related events are not yet prominent in Western records, though the disease was already spreading in Central Asia and the Middle East.

graph_edges.note: Both events occurred in 1334 in different regions; no direct causal link
Zhu Yuanzhang's early military service led to his rise to power in the 1350s–1360s
